concepts.lesson: India is a vast country with a wide variety of landforms such as mountains, plains, plateaus, deserts, coastal plains, and islands. These diverse physical features influence climate, agriculture, and settlement patterns.
concepts.real_example: The Himalayan Mountains in the north and the coastal plains in the south show India’s physical diversity.
key_points: Includes mountains, plains, plateaus, deserts, coasts and islands

concepts.lesson: India’s physical features are grouped into six major physiographic divisions: the Himalayan Mountains, the Northern Plains, the Peninsular Plateau, the Indian Desert, the Coastal Plains, and the Islands.
concepts.real_example: The Northern Plains stretch across northern India, while the Peninsular Plateau covers most of southern India.

concepts.lesson: The Himalayas are young fold mountains formed by tectonic activity. They stretch from the Indus river in the west to the Brahmaputra river in the east and form the highest mountain barrier in the world.
concepts.real_example: Mount Kanchenjunga is one of the highest peaks in the Himalayas.
key_points: Highest mountain range in the world

concepts.lesson: The Himalayas consist of three parallel ranges: Himadri (Great Himalayas), Himachal (Lesser Himalayas), and Shiwaliks (Outer Himalayas). Each range differs in height, structure, and formation.
concepts.real_example: Hill stations like Shimla and Mussoorie are located in the Himachal range.
key_points: Himachal contains many valleys and hill stations

concepts.lesson: The Northern Plains are formed by the deposition of alluvium brought by the Indus, Ganga, and Brahmaputra river systems. These plains are fertile and densely populated.
concepts.real_example: The Ganga Plain supports large-scale agriculture such as wheat and rice cultivation.

concepts.lesson: The Northern Plains have distinct relief features such as Bhabar, Terai, Bhangar, and Khadar. These are formed by variations in river deposition and soil composition.
concepts.real_example: The Terai region is known for marshy land and dense forests.
key_points: Bhabar region has pebbles deposited by rivers
Terai is a marshy and wet region
Khadar is newer and fertile alluvial soil

concepts.lesson: The Peninsular Plateau is one of the oldest landmasses of the earth formed from igneous and metamorphic rocks. It is rich in mineral resources and has broad valleys and rounded hills.
concepts.real_example: The Chotanagpur Plateau is rich in minerals like coal and iron ore.
key_points: Formed by igneous and metamorphic rocks
Contains Central Highlands and Deccan Plateau

concepts.lesson: The Western Ghats and Eastern Ghats mark the edges of the Peninsular Plateau. The Western Ghats are higher and continuous, while the Eastern Ghats are lower and discontinuous.
concepts.real_example: Anai Mudi in the Western Ghats is one of the highest peaks in South India.

concepts.lesson: The Indian Desert lies in the western part of India and is characterized by sandy plains, sand dunes, and very low rainfall. Vegetation is sparse and climate is arid.
concepts.real_example: The Thar Desert in Rajasthan is the largest desert region in India.

concepts.lesson: The Coastal Plains lie along the Arabian Sea in the west and the Bay of Bengal in the east. These plains are important for trade, fishing, and agriculture.
concepts.real_example: The Malabar Coast is part of the western coastal plains.
key_points: Western coast includes Konkan and Malabar
Eastern coast includes Northern Circar and Coromandel

concepts.lesson: India has two main island groups: the Lakshadweep Islands in the Arabian Sea and the Andaman and Nicobar Islands in the Bay of Bengal. These islands have unique ecosystems and strategic importance.
concepts.real_example: Lakshadweep islands are formed by coral deposits.
key_points: Andaman and Nicobar are volcanic origin

concepts.lesson: The physical features of India support various economic activities such as agriculture, mining, fishing, and trade. Each landform contributes to the development of the country.
concepts.real_example: The Northern Plains are the granaries of India due to fertile soil and water supply.
